Xbox Save Game Corruption Tool (XbSGCorrupt)

The Xbox Save Game Corruption (XbSGCorrupt) tool produces corrupted copies of a specified save game file for use in testing how a game title deals with corrupted data.

Usage

USAGE: xbsgcorrupt [options] savegamefile [destination]
Option Meaning
/Help or /? Display usage message.
/NoLogo Suppress copyright notice.
/Verbose Print details about what XbSGCorrupt is doing to the save game file.
/HeaderNULLBodyZeroLength Make a 24-byte file consisting of NULLs. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 24 bytes in length.)
/HeaderZeroLength Remove the first 24 bytes from the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 24 bytes in length.)
/HeaderNULL Place NULLs in the first 24 bytes of the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 25 bytes in length.)
/HeaderFileSizeWrong Change the contents of the first 4 bytes of the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 4 bytes in length.)
/HeaderHashWrong Change the contents of bytes 4 through 19 (zero index). (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 24 bytes in length.)
/BodyZeroLength Truncates the file to 24 bytes in length. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 25 bytes in length.)
/BodyAddHead Insert 4 random bytes at a point 24 bytes into the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 24 bytes in length.)
/BodyInsert Insert 4 bytes randomly between the 24th byte and the end of the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 25 bytes in length.)
/BodyRandomChange Randomly change 4 consecutive bytes between the 24th byte and the end of the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 28 bytes in length.)
/FilePeriodicChange Randomly change 4 consecutive bytes for every 16 KB of the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 16,384 bytes in length.)
/FileAddTail Add 10 MB to the end of the file.
/FileZeroLength Make a zero length file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is already a zero length file.)
/RemoveHashAtTail Remove the last 20 bytes from the file. (This option is skipped if savegamefile is less than 20 bytes in length.)

If you specify more than one corruption option on the command line, XbSGCorrupt creates multiple corrupted copies of the original file. Each copy is located in its own subdirectory of the destination directory specified on the command line. The corrupted save game file itself has the same file name as the original file. As an example, consider the following command line:

xbsgcorrupt /BodyInsert /BodyRandomChange SaveGame1.dat c:\corrupt

Assuming that SaveGame1.dat is at least 24 bytes long, the preceding command line produces the following files:

c:\corrupt\BodyInsert\SaveGame1.dat
c:\corrupt\BodyRandomChange\SaveGame1.dat

Without any options specified, XbSGCorrupt acts as if all the options have been specified, producing corrupt save games for every option that makes sense with the supplied save game file. Not all options are valid with a particular file; depending on the contents and length of the save game file, XbSGCorrupt may skip some options.

If no destination directory is specified, XbSGCorrupt writes its output to subdirectories of the current directory. If the destination directory specified does not exist, XbSGCorrupt creates it.

Remarks

The XbSGCorrupt tool is intended for corrupting save games for certification testing. Use the following steps to test how your game title deals with corrupt save game files:

  1. Using XbCp or the Shell Extension (Xbox Neighborhood), copy a save game file from the Xbox Development Kit to your development system.
  2. Run XbSGCorrupt on the save game file to produce one or more corrupted versions of the save game file.
  3. Copy each corrupt save game file back to the Xbox console, and then load each in the game to perform certification testing.
Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
